transcript.transcript: I got, you know, after years later, I became a very nice kid. As a kid I wasn't that nice. But the, you know, the park, the driveway system in the park is pretty much as it is today and the big Avenue of Elms which actually started at 9th Street Capitol Hill is now Capitol Boulevard across 7th Street I'm not even sure that 7th Street was open that far. You've got that Avenue of Elms right through there. And, you know, there's quite a lot of wells, you know, and a lot of speculation about how far the wind could, some of the wind should have been blown. How far was it? The avenue of Elms, the street that goes through the park. Oh. Yeah, It ended at the zoo, like it does today. But it actually started at 9th Street. That was my recollection. Do you remember when the elms were planted? I mean, or had that been done before you became conscious? All I remember is that they were there. Well and by well, by the time you were 10, it would be 1935 or so, by that time Capitol Boulevard, Memorial Boulevard, or whatever it was called then, it was built. And so those elms sort of disappeared. Yeah, when they opened up 7th Street all through there. And it became Capitol Boulevard. They changed the name to Capitol Boulevard. I don't have a good chronology. And I don't know what the city maps show. Obviously, there would be city maps that would show how something was opened up. But I remember clearly that, you know, with the construction of the bridge, which was a big amenity for the park, too. The creation of Capitol Boulevard, of course, made the art museum location significant. And also the State Historical Museum location was determined not long after that. Just prior to World War II, the country was under World War II, the Historical Museum was started and then construction suspended until after the war. Now I remember the parade, the celebration of the opening of the bridge. Watching it, and China, we went down and saw the parade someplace near Grove Street, someplace between Grove and Main. I remember the Chinese particularly. There were trees along there in those days. There were some trees, and not real big trees. But the Chinese had hung a great string of firecrackers on all these trees. And there were got a huge bunch of firecrackers and set them off and that was really... I still can see those firecrackers going off and flying through the air. That night my father enjoyed that too question? Well that the way they celebrated the way they opened that bridge and the way it was dedicated to the pioneers of the Oregon Trail. The effort they made to bring the pioneers that still remained to the proper conveyances and cars and buggies and what have you. It was very touching. And they had, they invited the Indian Native Americans. I don't know if the Chinese were in the parade or not, but I think they were maybe present, as you say. Well, they had their buildings there, you know. Yeah, tell me about what you remember about the Chinese buildings. Along the... Where were they? Around the fringes of the park? No, they were along Front Street and 7th Street. And these were commercial buildings? Sure. A couple of them were designed by, there were substantial buildings. There was two of them at least designed by Tourtellote and Hummel. And there's a couple of them still there. Where the cleaning company is, American Cleaning. That's what's left. That's the last remaining Chinese building down there. And by Chinese you mean they were commissioned by Chinese people. Oh yeah, they owned and operated by Chinese residents of the city. There were several families, and they particularly those along Front Street from 6th to Capitol Boulevard, then on from Grove to Front, to... From Grove to Main, up to the alley between Main and Grove, there was Chinese buildings. And across 7th Street, west side of 7th Street, in that block from Grove to the front, were several two-story Chinese buildings. There's photographs on them. One photograph in particular with the Chinese Republic and American flags on the front and some people in the balcony. Those were, you know they had Chinese doctors. yeah Herb doctors and they sold stuff. Eell the reason I asked you if there were residents particularly is because Ralph my husband recalls his buddy he lived on the other side of the river, I don't know if you ever ran into him in the park or not, he was a little, slightly younger than you, by four years, so it's only slight. But he and his buddies would catch fish and sell them to the Chinese. And I'm not the one who interviewed him, so maybe he described exactly where they would perfect these transactions. So where they took the fish, I don't know. Well, you, you know, there was the Chinatown. It was, it was, I think, I don't remember people calling it Chinatown, but it was a little Chinatown, you know. And there was, you know, there was another, there were buildings also, a few buildings just west of Capitol Boulevard on the front. There were quite a few Chinese living there. I mean, not all necessarily in those buildings, but in other places. And, of course they were operating the gardens in what's now Garden City along the river west of 16th Street. Maybe they rode their bicycles up there and came to fish. Yeah, sure. He apparently remembers fishing the river along the park. Uh-huh, yeah, sure. Sure, well, you know, the Chinese people were right there. I mean, the buildings and everything. Did you fish? No. I wasn't really into fishing. I was in scouts, boy scouts. But fishing wasn't one of those things that I was interested in. Did the scouts have events or activities at the park? Not particularly. There were various kinds of camping demonstrations and stuff like that, but I don't remember them actually at Julie Davis Park. But the other things at Julie Davis, you know, well... You know, it was a wonderful place to watch birds. Of course, there weren't as many, there was a lot of more diversity of water birds than there are today, because there were actually two ponds. And you can tell where the ponds were linked by that kind of ditch. And the pond that's behind, the pond that runs, meanders behind the zoo. So you actually crossed a park street and you can tell where there was a bridge across that pond. I think the balustrades of that bridge are still there. And the pond then extended out into the west of the part of the park where there's a picnic shelter now. And it was a kind of a boggy pond with a lot of trees around it, and extended about halfway towards the banjo. It was narrow. It had sort of the character of that same slough that's behind the zoo. But it was definitely a kind of a slough. It was fed, I guess, by the water from the gravel pond, you know, up to the river level, or higher. And I particularly remember that other sort of slough there in that part of the park was particularly attractive to wood duck. Very pretty duck. And there was a lot of, a lot of wood duck, a big flock of wood duck would winter there. Of course a lot of mallards and teal and not as many geese as we have today. The geese kind of... The geese became a big thing in Van Morrison Park. People started feeding them. Now we have this resident population of geese there. Well, when I was at Boise Junior College, there were a lot of geese on the campus. Ralph probably remembers that too. They left those walks in the wintertime because there were steam tunnels. Yeah. There It's a bunch of goose poop out of me, but I understand the walk-on. Tell me a little bit about, we've talked about this before, but not on tape, the, when the, When the Chamber of Commerce and others decided they wanted to promote having the interstate connector come through town, how did you – tell me about your involvement in that whole effort. I think you were, you had an opinion about that and acted on it at the time. Actually, I was a little bit on the sidelines of that dispute. And, of course, along with my father. My father, you know, was very active and was on the City Planning and Zoning Commission for years. In fact, I kind of succeeded to concede on that, on the Zoning Commission. The Zoning Commission and the Planning Commission were separate bodies and the Planning Commission had been more of them for years. And, In what period of time are we talking about? Right in the 19... The period of 1953 to 1960. The period 1953 to 1960. And of course I was busy at work learning how to be an architect in the Hummel firm. And I had to mind my Ps and Qs because my father, my uncle, and Jed Jones were the partners. Partners. We had work and we had a lot of time in the office. Well, the interstate of course came through, you know, when? The interstate got built across where it is today. And it wasn't complete. Came in, as I recall it, came in from the west, from the coast. About as far as, what's the name of the Broadway connector? About Cole. Yeah, something in there. And then just Highway 30 from then on, we got building segments like that. But it wasn't very long before this question of how is it going to access the city got put up for consideration by the Highway Department and they had, as you know, several alternatives and wanted to bring it more or less parallel to the railroad connection into town, into downtown. And in fact it probably would have done that. Well, along where it is now, sort of cross country, along Franklin Road, down into town. But once it got to the Fairview Hill, that was the question then, what was it going to do? And The highway department's initial scheme was to bring it right along the riverbank. All the way through town along the river bank. And... The North Bank. The North Bank, right across... Across 16th Street hadn't yet been extended off to the bench. You got to Morris Hill Cemetery by, on 9th Street and Crescent Rim, and you know, right along the Riverbank, right through Julia Davis Park, for God's sake, and out to Broadway. I don't know what, from that point on, I frankly don't remember what the alignment was supposed to have been, but I suppose it would have crossed Broadway and then hang across the river someplace and across south, you know, that eastern part of South Boise, back up onto the bench, and would have realigned itself with basically the Highway 30 right of way, which largely it was. The principal was built along the Highway 30 right of way. That controversy just split the town in half. I remember, to my surprise, Jed Jones, who's one of the partners of my father and my uncle, thought it was fine. And my father and my uncle and I were, to the extent that they deigned to talk to me, I wasn't the fair-haired boy in that office, I was just another draftsman. They thought it was atrocious. And my uncle in particular was quite a sensitive man when it comes to gardening, parks, and things of that kind. He was just aghast that that was one thing he proposed. And, of course, eventually the highway department agreed reluctantly that it had to at least cross Capitol Boulevard, 9th Street and Capitol Boulevard, with an overpass, and not at a grade level crossing. And I think the model for it was what the highway was, was Spokane, you know, where the interstate came right down through the middle of Spokane, along the bottom of the South Hill, along the, you know, across the, on the south side of the river, and built interchanges into the Spokane business district. Along there are several of them. And of course, for Spokane, that was a big deal. It worked. Why did your father and uncle object? Why did they think it was atrocious? Because they were offended by the idea of it, doing that to the river, knocking out the river. They were fond of the river because they grew up not far from the river, you know, South 13th Street. Not so much the park. Well, I think they had a feeling for the park, too. Yeah, sure. Yeah, they thought that it was, You know, it would have had a terrible impact on everything along the way. Sure. And, so that was... So how did they, did they get involved? Not... I think they expressed their opinion in various ways, but I'm not sure they did it locally, publicly, but their opinion and comment I think about it. To tell you the truth, Susan, I don't remember exactly what turned the tide. I got on the Planning and Zoning Commission about 1960, and it was Mayor Shelworth that appointed me to the Planning Commission and I think that the at the time the controversy was already over by 1960. And the decision was that there would be a spur from the interstate heading towards town along the same alignment where the I-84 connector is today. And then of course, as you know, we just got to the top of Fairview Avenue, just stopped there. And then the rest of the interstate would be linked up on its present alignment out there by the airport. And you know, now that I mention the airport, my uncle Frank was on the airport commission and the office did the first airport terminal building at their ground downfield. He had a big interest in the airport. He and Wes Willick and a number of other prominent businessmen were on the Airport Commission. And they liked the idea of the interstate being by the airport. That that made sense in terms of its transportation potential for various reasons. I think that may have been one of the reasons why Frank was particularly unhappy about the idea of the third county through town. About the idea of the, of coming through town. But I, in Hootsburg, by that time I think I was active in the chamber. Should we close that door? Yeah, I'd better. It's on the right side. Because I was on, I think I was maybe even sitting on the Transportation Committee of the Chamber if they had one, but in any event, go ahead. I've eaten mine too. We can have more. I remember distinctly going out to the dedication of the opening, of the complete opening of the interstate out there on the bench. The last link, which is the bridge overpass where the railroad track crosses the Interstate, just a little bit further east than the Broadway, about a half a mile east of the Broadway interchange. You know, there's a place there. The old rock kind of arrives there and it's a fairly substantial crossing. And that was the last thing that had to be built. And when they had a ribbon cutting for that, that opened the interstate completely. And Ellis Mathis was the chief engineer for the, whatever title, for the highway department. A very nice guy. But he was, because the high command of the highway department were disgusted that that, that they didn't get their way. And I remember His comments on this opening was sort of to the effect that, well, Boise, this is what you wanted, so here it is. Kind of like, sort of in that tone. You're not gonna like it, but this is what you got, this is what you asked for. I thought, oh well. I'm sure that was Allison Atkins, in ATHES. I remember. He was a nice guy, but he and the chief cooks and bottle washers of the highway department were really unhappy. And, of course, the connector didn't make it into downtown until for another 30 years, almost. Right. And wouldn't have made it then in such a timely way, at least, if it hadn't been, for God's sake, for Steve Sims. You probably heard that before. Boise Planning and Development Commission, Boise Planning and Development Committee, which I was a member of, you know, lobbied real hard to get the connector completed. And they persuaded Steve Sims, who was about as useful as tits in a pool in Congress, to get one of those famous, what do they call them, earmarks or something. Ork. Yeah. Ork earmarks. He sat, actually, Steve Sims sat on the Transportation Committee of the Senate. So he, you know, he was in a powerful position. And I don't know what else he did that was significant, but that was pretty significant. And I think you... I can remember that. Yeah, right. But that's what made it possible. And then of course, BRA. And it was with BRA too, because I was all mixed up with the LA crap. Fighting Domtown and everything, trying to save downtown from this wonderful shopping center. Or whatever was going to happen downtown. But even with that, you know, the completion of the connector as it is today, really is an artifact of the ERA project. There was a loop around, you know, and some other kind of things that had to be done. Right. I remember myself thinking of it as the highway to the underground parking lot underneath one quarter of all downtown. Oh yeah, right. People were gonna get off, they were gonna come on the connector, the interchange or the exit would lead them directly to the parking lot. Escalators would bring them up to the marketing floor. Oh yeah, right. And they would pour their regional money into our downtown. There you go. That was how I thought of it. Sure, right. It was so, in my way, my way of looking at it as a planner. Basically, that was the scheme. That was the whole point. Sure. Well, did the agency, your agency, get involved in the interstate? Or was that before it was created? Oh, you mean the back? Well, I don't know. You mean the interstate proper? Yeah. I don't know. I didn't arrive on the scene until 1973, and the interstate was... Yeah, and of course, the agency was created in response to the Highway Act. Is it... I don't know if I'm right in my head. The... Oh, ACOG. ACOG. Theater Council of Camerons. Right. It was a product of the requirement that there be regional regional plan to just to participate in getting certain kinds of transportation. Right. Sometime I will try to figure out just what it was that made winners out of one side on that park issue and losers on the other. The park? I mean the connector through the park. Oh, the connector. Thank you for watching.

story.title: Hummel_Charles_F_OH 2703 Side B
story.description: Recorded on December 6, 2006. Topic is Julia Davis Park.
